summary:
The Pottery Neolithic of the Levant, dated broadly from approximately 7000 to 5500 BCE, marks the transition from the aceramic Neolithic communities of the Pre-Pottery Neolithic B period to cultures using fired clay vessels as a routine part of daily life. This transition was neither sudden nor uniform across the Levant — different communities adopted pottery at different rates and developed distinct regional ceramic traditions — but the appearance of pottery broadly correlates with other changes in social organization, settlement pattern, and economy.

The Pottery Neolithic is associated with a shift from the large, densely settled villages of the earlier Pre-Pottery Neolithic B toward somewhat smaller and more dispersed communities, though the reasons for this demographic change remain debated. Agricultural practices were well established by this time, with communities cultivating [[event:14|wheat and barley]] alongside legumes and managing herds of domesticated cattle, sheep, goats, and pigs. The introduction of pottery facilitated more efficient food processing, particularly the boiling of grains and legumes, and enabled longer-term storage of liquids including dairy products.

Regional Pottery Neolithic cultures in the Levant include the Wadi Rabah culture of the Jordan Valley and adjacent highlands, the Byblosian and related coastal traditions, and several other regional variants. The period also witnesses continued use of obsidian tools obtained through long-distance exchange networks, suggesting that Pottery Neolithic communities maintained broad trade connections across the Near East. The [[event:1402|Pottery Neolithic]] transition in the Levant thus represents both a technological change and a social realignment that paved the way for the Chalcolithic developments of the fifth and fourth millennia BCE.
